title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee meeting covered CIP updates including a $785,000 DOT grant for Birch Street reconstruction, approved a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines of $100 per day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road and consultant services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkkey Avenue
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. This 2029 project will likely be moved up to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for pavement settlement issues, and upcoming restoration work on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street projects.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, spring sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant flooding required dewatering operations along the Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week. Discussion focused on developing a permanent pump solution for recurring flooding issues in the Zenzer/Shorey Avenue area. Culverts failed on Jones Street requiring additional repairs. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed.

Josh reported on service repairs, well maintenance, and lift station issues including power and phase failures. A major concern was identified: April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the distribution system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water infiltration/inflow. Staff identified residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. The In-pipe Technologies INI study will help identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing net operating income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss is at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total. The committee acknowledged the report unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for state transportation. The resolution highlights that recurring annual transportation funding remains inadequate, requiring communities to subsidize with general tax dollars or implement wheel taxes. The committee recommended approval to the village board unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled material at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23 per ton for an estimated 30,000 tons, totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated the program saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. The committee unanimously approved awarding the contract to King Gravel.

Staff sought guidance on enforcing sewer ordinance violations for illegal sump pump discharges. Current code allows fines between $100 and $1,000 per day. Trustee Jordan distinguished between unknowing and willful violations. The committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines not to exceed $100 per day, allowing time for the In-pipe Technologies study to identify problem areas before issuing violation notices. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement for Radkkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a new duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect via long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee members requested adding specific language that reconstruction occur no later than the 2029 construction season. The agreement requires the property owner to pay assessments when mains are extended. Motion passed unanimously with the added date requirement.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share for utility relocation is estimated at $94,860 ($61,560 for sanitary sewer, $88,740 for water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Water main relocation is required due to conflicts with storm sewer improvements. The committee unanimously recommended approval to the village board.

Michael presented consultant proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project, MSA was selected at $37,315 (lower than Clark Dietz's $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 despite being slightly higher than MSA's $70,400 because Clark Dietz proposed full-time staff rather than a summer intern. Both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: April 13, 2026 meeting minutes approved unanimously
April 2026 water and sewer permits acknowledged unanimously
2025 Water Utility PSC annual report acknowledged unanimously
Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding recommended for board approval unanimously
2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract awarded to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900) unanimously
Staff directed to create sanitary sewer discharge violation enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100 per day
Water and sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue approved with reconstruction deadline of no later than 2029
Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild approved with Weston share of $94,860
MSA selected for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout construction services at $37,315
Clark Dietz selected for Bladel Avenue construction services at $73,730
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM for Bladel Avenue project
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission also heard that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield has been canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: The commission elected officers for the reorganized board. Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as secretary.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield hot air balloon event has been canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commission member noted the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage from the Holiday Inn Express where pilots stay. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with Jackson seconding the motion. Passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting winner Anastasia had three bachelor's degrees and attended South Dakota State. The candidates toured Marathon County including Mullen's Cheese, marking the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. Renee noted the candidates were thankful for the community's hospitality.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest requested to provide accurate hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with staff conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff remarks

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: The commission reviewed the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Staff reported the project meets all B2 district zoning requirements. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to address a gas pipeline easement issue, with the parking lot now over the 36-inch deep gas line, which is acceptable to the gas company. Discussion included questions about future expandability (possible to the north), landscaping meeting current code, and placement of an alternate storage shed. A motion to approve with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff presented a program for two annual inspections of salt storage facilities in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and drainage, and one winter inspection focused on operations and containment. The program uses a standardized worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Discussion revealed one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and staff will reach out to them. A motion to approve the program with reporting back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 Plan Commission meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing; staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston spoke via remote connection. He requested the commission urge staff to waive excessive landscaping requirements for the police department project similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also requested annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get lost.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive approved with staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-annual inspections
Staff to report back to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee on salt shed inspection findings next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
First joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations to be discussed at future meeting in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, approving April 1st meeting minutes and receiving educational presentations from department heads about the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No substantive policy actions were taken beyond the minutes approval.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting Supervisors Kremes and Vice Chair Rosenberg attending remotely)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure for new members
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried with all in favor voting 'aye' with no opposition.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, communicable disease prevention including recent tick awareness efforts, Nurse Family Partnership program graduating its first two-year-olds, and the new homeless system response position created by the previous board's task force. The department has approximately 37.6 FTE across four teams: environmental health, nursing, community health improvement, and administration.

Administrator Lance Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa (not present). He explained the department's 140 FTE structure covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing root causes of housing instability, noting many participants had never had credit accounts, bank accounts, or formal leases. He mentioned potential restructuring requests may come for increased staffing needs.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo presented on the 3.0 FTE department serving veterans and their dependents. He reported 8,149 interactions in 2025 (up from 5,900 in 2024), $44 million annually coming to local veterans, and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits outreach, housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). The Man of Honor Society provided $116,000 to local veterans in 2025.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county Aging & Disability Resource Center serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. He described services including information assistance, meals on wheels nutrition program, falls prevention, Medicare/Medicaid benefits counseling, and caregiver support. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remains flat, requiring meal and staffing reductions. The organization has about 60 FTEs and relies on 385-400 volunteers.

actionItems: April 1st committee meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Next meeting confirmed for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Committee members invited to submit agenda item requests via email to Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard
Strategic plan review to be presented at upcoming meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed ordinances on mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made appointments to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public comments - Jim Pitel on engineering consultants and committee appointments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances - mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, e-bikes
Discussion on remote meeting attendance policy amendments
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, $250 per duplex to $563, and multifamily to $495, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel questioned the lack of detailed cost data justifying the fees. Trustee Motti supported the increase noting population growth requires more park infrastructure. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously with motion by Lopes Sorrell and second by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously with motion by Hermling and second by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional reciprocity agreement between Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, and Riverside for fire inspections of food trucks. Once inspected by one participating municipality, a sticker indicates compliance for all, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously with motion by Lopes Sorrell and second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the policy. Trustees agreed to remove language requiring specific justifications like health, safety, or welfare for remote attendance, keeping the policy broad. Members must notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and cannot participate in closed sessions. Trustee Beck moved to amend with these strikethroughs, seconded by Clark. The amendment passed with two nos from Hermling and Motti. The original motion as amended passed with the same two dissenting votes.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes Sorrell, approved unanimously. The board also agreed to include applicant resumes in future board packets for transparency.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue location to development. Concerns included parking congestion with vehicles on grass areas, safety issues, and field damage. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor stalls. The board considered but rejected Mont Miller Park due to its remote location. No action was taken; this was discussion only. Renee clarified the market operates at a profit through vendor fees, grants, and reimbursements.

Village Engineer Michael Dolan reported that DOT engineering studies showed the road should be 35 mph, not 45. A traffic study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph. He needs to complete a second study after construction ends to potentially justify changing the posted limit. A resident noted the speed has been inconsistent with the ordinance for over two months.

In response to Jim Pitel's public comment about unfilled positions, Michael Dolan explained the engineering technician position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set is scarce in central Wisconsin, with even consulting firms struggling to find local candidates.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of Deon 52 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants ($108,000 estimated) when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a Safer board meeting before being officially appointed and criticized the lack of information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet. He also commented during the impact fee public hearing questioning the lack of detailed operational cost data to justify the fee amounts.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Ordinance 26-010 approved increasing park and recreation impact fees
Resolution 2026-015 approved amending village fee schedule for impact fees
Ordinance 26-008 approved for mobile food vendor license regional reciprocity
Ordinance 26-009 approved amending standing committees
Ordinance 26-011 approved amending nuisances chapter
Ordinance 26-012 approved amending bicycle regulations for e-bikes
Resolution 2026-014 adopted supporting state transportation fund solutions
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 pm
Staff to bring Weston Avenue speed limit issue back to the board
Staff to prepare intergovernmental agreement for building inspection services to Town of Ringle

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board heard emotional public testimony from affected Ringle residents requesting fee relief.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Vote on rules suspension and tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments including increasing diversity of perspective, providing leadership development opportunities for newer supervisors (34% of the board is in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. Vice Chair Boots assisted in developing the appointments. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimously.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for 'our county's smallest children and our oldest elders.' Paper ballots were distributed for the vote, and Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fees modification for storm debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal to reduce tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle Township waived its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing its rate to $36.94 per ton (covering costs only). Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck from the affected Ringle community spoke, requesting the county fully waive fees and extend the timeline to six months. Multiple supervisors asked questions about implementation, certification processes, and potential costs. The board unanimously approved suspending rules to bring the resolution forward, then unanimously passed Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty argued Marathon County should waive fees entirely like other entities have done, noting private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup costs and residents are facing deductibles and uninsured losses. She requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, requesting fee waivers for underinsured residents and noting some insurance adjusters haven't yet visited the approximately 44 affected homes.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved for the term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved amending social services budget
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified storm debris through June 17th
Administrator to continue discussions with Community Foundation about reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop a formal policy within six months for handling similar disaster situations in the future

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board that will only waive reconnect fees for septic systems (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Introduction to committee roles and responsibilities
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; staff explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make these conforming uses. The Town Board recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacted properties
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and pouts fees for properties damaged by the April 17th tornado in the towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concerns about fairness to property owners impacted by disasters not declared emergencies. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to forward the resolution to the county board. Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees for pouts systems, not other zoning or pouts fees. Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment carried. The amended motion to forward to county board passed, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher presented on the department's operations, explaining they accept about 1,000 tons of waste daily with 11 staff members. He highlighted the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, significant capital expenses for landfill expansion and closure, and upcoming requirements by 2032 for leachate treatment due to PFAS concerns. Marilyn Bend commended the department for keeping independent landfills competitive.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He noted the department serves both Marathon County and City of Wausau with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff. He highlighted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years with the county, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (Green Valley) approved and forwarded to county board
Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (McMillan) approved and forwarded to county board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for April 17, 2026 tornado-impacted properties forwarded to county board
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for potential future policy development
Conservation services educational presentation scheduled for next meeting with Kirsty Heiden

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved installation of kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River Trail, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and the Yellow Banks disc golf course. Public comment included opposition to dogs in the pool and criticism of the 4 p.m. meeting time and Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held.

Sean Oster (Director) presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception in 2023 to the present, including the recent May 9th ribbon cutting for Ray Jack and Western Fields. He explained that Friends of Kennedy Park is fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently having approximately $84-85,000 of the $117,000 goal at the community foundation. A public announcement with a check presentation is planned for their June tournament. Scott asked about village capital dollars, and Sean confirmed no village funds are currently earmarked pending the master plan's development. Committee members emphasized that master plans are guides subject to change and that the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos and maps showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during logging work. The mats were installed by TC Energy (formerly ANR Pipeline/ATC) and the village retained some extra mats for future trail projects. The equipment access required building snow bridges in winter conditions.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to next month because Katrina, who requested the discussion, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. Andy, a resident who spoke during public comment opposing dogs in the pool, was informed his objection was noted and he was invited to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The committee discussed challenges removing graffiti from concrete benches and structures along the trail. Sean explained that pressure washing equipment cannot access the trail due to narrow bridges and steep ditches. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, clear sealers, and volunteer work days. No formal action was taken, but staff will explore cleaning solutions. Committee member Scott noted people might volunteer to help if asked.

Dan Borchardt from Marathon County presented version 7 of proposed signs for kayak launches along the Eau Claire River. Signs will be installed at launches 3, 4, and 5 in Weston (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). The signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific history panels. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. The committee approved a motion to install the signs and forward to the village board. Motion carried unanimously.

Sean raised a request from an individual about installing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting one in 13 people have food allergies. The committee agreed to bring this back for future discussion and education. Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026.

Sean reported that dugout painting at Kennedy Park was completed, aquatic center pool repairs by the builder were finished the previous Tuesday, and pool filling was underway. Opening day is June 6th with training this weekend and rentals starting next Wednesday. He also announced the Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler and a final bike/pedestrian plan open house on June 11th from 5-7 p.m.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), participating online under the name 'Vern M', criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as demonstrating 'zero desire for interaction and input from the general public.' He also questioned Kennedy Park financials, calling the $8,700 expense figure 'too coincidental' and asking where grant money and glow event proceeds are, stating the numbers are 'extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22, 2026 meeting
Approved installation of kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J launches - forwarded to village board
Staff to explore graffiti removal solutions and anti-vandalism coatings for Eau Claire River Trail
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future committee discussion
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan final open house June 11, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held a routine meeting approving April budget and financial reports, and received an educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving out of Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.body: Motion to approve minutes from the previous Finance and HR Committee meeting was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIFF closure. Staff explained they anticipate calling the bonds in October/November, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the village will be able to pay off the debt as planned.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. The motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations, staffing (now 2 full-time and 1 part-time employee, down from 4 full-time previously), elections administration including Badger Book equipment purchases ($11,000 for four units), licensing (51 liquor licenses, pet licensing brought back in-house saving approximately $55/year in third-party fees), and tax collection (84% or $22 million collected at the counter). Discussion included pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 driven by Marathon County's increased fees to the Humane Society, and newsletter costs of approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well over the past three Saturdays. Vendors are happy with the new setup and location. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft vendors, and some produce vendors with greenhouses. Committee members discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff noting the west side of Alderson now allows parking while the east side remains no parking.

Evan announced this would be his last meeting as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked the committee for the opportunity to serve. The chair noted there are now open spots on this committee and others, encouraging members to reach out to interested citizens.

actionItems: Minutes from previous meeting approved
April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution to call TIFF bonds expected this summer for October payoff
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots including Evan's vacancy
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to nuisance ordinances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized demolition proceedings for an abandoned property. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both items for further research.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Written communications and department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
Crosswalk request on Alderson Street near Mount Olive Church
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Code Enforcement Officer Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement list after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt noted the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), issued 281 citations, and made 410 traffic stops. The department has hired its 40th officer. Two SROs work summer zoning violations for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Reports were acknowledged unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements of 14 feet over streets and 10 feet over walkways. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees. Passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the model e-bike ordinance with significant debate about emotos (electric dirt bikes). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether electric scooters should be exempt for children. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Passed unanimously. Rothschild and other municipalities are also considering adoption; Schofield declined; Wausau is making adjustments to their existing ordinance.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour no-parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most have 4-5 hour windows). Public Works Director Michael Wodalski suggested eliminating overnight parking restrictions entirely and signing only 6-8 problem areas. Chief Hunt opposed elimination, preferring overnight restrictions to clear roads before plows arrive. Committee deferred action until July, directing staff to identify specific problem areas for targeted signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk connecting their parking to DC Everest High School administrative lot across Alderson Street. Committee discussed whether village should bear cost ($2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system). Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval contingent on Mount Olive covering installation costs. Passed unanimously. Staff will contact the church about cost-sharing.

Fire Chief explained new regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive stickers valid in all jurisdictions. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing second address), properties on Ross Avenue out of sequence, and 2211 Radkey Avenue near new duplex development. Chief Hunt noted additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments where each unit has separate address. Motion passed unanimously to recommend address corrections to the Board.

Staff requested authorization to proceed with demolition of abandoned structure vacant since 2010. Fire department previously declined training burn due to unsafe conditions. Current owners previously housed chickens in the structure. Owners prefer demolition with costs added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to authorize raze order. Passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding multiple agenda items: criticized the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking; questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt; asked why fire inspections were being added for mobile food vendors; noted the new public property location changes food truck permitting dynamics; and expressed concern about old code violations on the enforcement list that appear to have no visible progress after years.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to Board with amendment prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Public Works Director to identify 6-8 specific areas for targeted parking restrictions and bring back to July meeting
Staff to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 establishing regional mobile food vendor fire inspection program
Recommend address corrections for E911 compliance to Board of Trustees
Authorize staff to begin raze order proceedings for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition complaint at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
